interactions.summary: Jiang uses the sky-is-blue analogy to explain why Muhammad's monotheism could feel liberating: an authority confirms what people already felt about God, and God becomes everywhere, touchable, and knowable.

He defines Islam's power as the fusion of pagan concreteness with monotheistic simplicity, then calls Islam the first modern monotheism, the portal of modernity, and the origin of ideas later associated with Protestantism and science.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang lecture with non-substantive room marker

speaker_notes.note: The single 'Okay?' labeled UNKNOWN is a non-substantive room marker and not a question to surface.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that Muhammad's message confirmed for many persecuted believers that their own perception of God had been right all along.
Islam's modern form of monotheism makes God everywhere, visible, touchable, and internally present.
The beauty of Islam, for Jiang, is that it joins pagan intimacy and concreteness to monotheism's clarity and absoluteness.
claims.temporal_scope: Comparative religious model in this lecture

claims.claim: Jiang says Islam is the portal of modernity because it already contains ideas later associated with Protestantism and science: God can be in us, and God expects us to improve the world.

signature_moments.moment: The sky-is-blue analogy turns theology into lived coercion: people are told to distrust their own eyes until Muhammad authorizes what they already know.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we've been lying to you all along, the sky is blue
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives emotional force to why a clear monotheism could become revolutionary.

signature_moments.moment: Islam is not merely premodern in Jiang's account; it is the portal through which modernity enters history.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Islam really is the portal of modernity
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is one of the lecture's strongest reversible claims against Europe-centered modernization stories.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's name for Islam's concrete, absolute, everywhere-present God, which becomes how modern people imagine monotheism.
Jiang's claim that Islam carries foundational modern ideas before Europe explicitly develops them.
The scientific-modern impulse Jiang links to God expecting humans to improve the world.

chronology_notes.note: Dated 2025-03-11: Jiang explicitly locates Protestant and scientific modernity's premises inside early Islam.
